coredata.dc:title: The heterogeneous nature of the lithium-ion diffusion in highly concentrated sulfolane-based liquid electrolytes

coredata.dc:description: Experimental data on Li-ion transport have indicated that cation hopping, or ligand-exchange mechanism contribute considerably to the Li ion conduction in highly concentrated electrolyte regimes. In this work we performed molecular dynamics simulations of solutions of LiBF4 in sulfolane, up to [LiBF4] = 5.8 mol.L−1 (mole fraction of sulfolane = 0.57), to better understand the solution structure and the nature of the Li ion diffusion. The radial distribution functions, aggregation and connectivity analyses revealed that as the concentration of salt increases, the Li ions can be pictured as nodes of a network shared by BF4 − and sulfolane molecules. The formation of these two different continuous networks sharing the cation seems to reach a sweet spot when the mole fraction of sulfolane is ca. 0.67, where the Li+ transference number achieves its maximum value in LiBF4–sulfolane mixtures. The autocorrelation functions of cation-anion and cation-sulfolane pair separations exhibit a non-exponential behaviour. Also, the relaxation dynamics of these solutions at and above room temperature recalls to the supercooled glass-forming liquids at temperatures near the glass transition. The relaxation process of the Li-anion pair is the major contributor to deviations from the Stokes-Einstein equation seen in the highly concentrated LiBF4–sulfolane solutions. These results showing the role played by the primary relaxation steps of Li ions in highly concentrated electrolytes can pave the way to innovative energy storage devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Selected radial distribution functions of the sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures calculated from MD simulation trajectories at T = 303 K as a function of sulfolane molar fraction (χSL).

Neighbour distribution functions of (a) O and (b) F atoms around Li+ cations as a function of the molar fraction of sulfolane χ SL. The distributions were calculated from the integration of Li-O and Li-F g(r)s. The vertical dashed line at r = 0.29 nm represents the end of the first solvation shell of the lithium cation, determined from the first minima in the g(r)s.

Selected weighted connectivity matrices between sulfolane molecules and BF4 − anions mediated by the Li+ ions as a function of χSL. The values depict the percentage of Li+ cations in solution coordinated with the other two species, calculated from MD trajectories stored over 10 ns. The criteria for accounting the SL—Li+ and BF4 −—Li+ bonding events were the first minimum in Li-O and Li-F g(r)s, ca. 0.29 nm in both cases.

(a) Total X-ray structure factors of LiBF4 solutions in sulfolane as a function of mole fraction of sulfolane; (b) contributions of SO2 and CH2 groups to the prepeak (4 ≤ q ≤ 8 nm−1); (c) partial structure factors of the components of the mixture showing the contributions to the intermediate peak (8 ≤ q ≤ 12 nm−1).
Aggregation analysis between Li+ ions and sulfolane molecules (top) and between Li+ cations and BF4 − anions (bottom).

Simulation snapshots depicting the polar networks of SL/LiBF4 mixtures, near the critical percolation concentrations for cation-anion and cation-sulfolane. The O, Li and F atoms are represented in red, dark yellow and green colours, respectively. For the sake of simplicity, only network nodes with Li+ ions shared simultaneously by O and F atoms are highlighted. Top left: χ SL = 0.57; top right: χ SL = 0.67; bottom left: χ SL = 0.75; bottom right: χ SL = 0.80.

Ratios of self-diffusion coefficients DSL/DLi and DBF4/DLi of sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures. Open symbols denote experimental values [1] at T = 303 K and filled symbols depict data from NVT MD simulations at T = 355 K.

Semi-log plots of the continuous dimer autocorrelation functions of the (a) cation-anion and (b) sulfolane-cation pairs, evaluated at 355 K from the MD trajectories as a function of the molar fraction of sulfolane χ SL.

Lithium transference number t Li taken from reference [1] as a function (a) of the average rate constant <k>, and (b) χ SL. Gray and black lines represent the linear regressions for Li+–sulfolane and Li+–BF4 − pairs, respectively, while the colours of the symbols depict the molar fraction of sulfolane χ SL for each data point.
Chemical structures and abbreviations of the compounds used in this work.

Molar fraction of sulfolane (χ SL), molar ratio SL/LiBF4, number of sulfolane molecules (n SL), LiBF4 pairs (n LiBF4), final box length at 303 K (l b, in nm), experimental and calculated densities (ρexp and ρMD , respectively, in g.cm−3) for sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures measured at 303 K.

head.ce:title: The heterogeneous nature of the lithium-ion diffusion in highly concentrated sulfolane-based liquid electrolytes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Experimental data on Li-ion transport have indicated that cation hopping, or ligand-exchange mechanism contribute considerably to the Li ion conduction in highly concentrated electrolyte regimes. In this work we performed molecular dynamics simulations of solutions of LiBF4 in sulfolane, up to [LiBF4] = 5.8 mol.L−1 (mole fraction of sulfolane = 0.57), to better understand the solution structure and the nature of the Li ion diffusion. The radial distribution functions, aggregation and connectivity analyses revealed that as the concentration of salt increases, the Li ions can be pictured as nodes of a network shared by BF4 − and sulfolane molecules. The formation of these two different continuous networks sharing the cation seems to reach a sweet spot when the mole fraction of sulfolane is ca. 0.67, where the Li+ transference number achieves its maximum value in LiBF4–sulfolane mixtures. The autocorrelation functions of cation-anion and cation-sulfolane pair separations exhibit a non-exponential behaviour. Also, the relaxation dynamics of these solutions at and above room temperature recalls to the supercooled glass-forming liquids at temperatures near the glass transition. The relaxation process of the Li-anion pair is the major contributor to deviations from the Stokes-Einstein equation seen in the highly concentrated LiBF4–sulfolane solutions. These results showing the role played by the primary relaxation steps of Li ions in highly concentrated electrolytes can pave the way to innovative energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since its inception 30 years ago, lithium-ion batteries (LIB) have become ubiquitous energy storage devices because of its advantageous qualities over its predecessors such as Ni metal hydride or lead-acid systems. However, volatile and flammable carbonate-based electrolytes are commonly employed, raising concerns on thermal stability and toxicity of the LIBs [1,2]. Also, the performance of conventional LIBs is somewhat limited by the low lithium transference number (t Li), defined as the fraction of the total current transported by the cation, with typical values about 0.4 [3]. Strategies such as the use of nonaqueous polyelectrolyte solutions [3], aqueous Li+-ion batteries [4], eutectic mixtures [2], superconcentrated or highly concentrated electrolyte (HCE) solutions [1] have received much attention recently to overcome the safety and environmental concerns about conventional LIBs. Regarding the HCE, the advantages over the conventional LIBs include the suppression of unfavourable side reactions, high thermal stability, wide electrochemical window, reduced vapor pressure, as well as increase in Li+-ion mass transfer. However, significant challenges still need to be overcome, such as the high viscosity of concentrated electrolytes and the formation of stable crystalline solvates [1,5–7].

From the HSAB principle [8], small neutral solutes likely experience diffusion through soft regions of the liquid dominated by solvent-solvent (nonpolar) interactions, while hard Lewis acids strongly solvated such as Li+ will likely diffuse through stiff parts of the liquid, dominated by solute-solvent (polar) interactions [9]. The deviations from the Stokes-Einstein equation, manifested as decoupling between diffusion and viscosity, arises when the solute and solvent have similar sizes. The charge distribution of the solute plays a role on solvent friction when the size ratio between the solute and solvent becomes small, and the heterogeneity of the solvent and its nanostructure also becomes relevant [9]. In HCE systems, a wealth of experimental work indicates that Li+ diffuses faster than solvent and anion, suggesting that Li-ion transport changes from cage diffusion to a jump-like, hopping conduction [1,5–7,9,10] reminiscent of the Grotthuss mechanism of proton transfer [11–13]. However, fundamental understanding of ion transport phenomena in these solutions is still limited.

Stretched exponential (or Kohlrausch–Williams–Watts function, KWW) kinetics has been observed in numerous studies, including relaxation processes in polymeric systems, spin glasses, electronic and molecular glasses [14], or protein folding [15]. Although the KWW function is often used simply as an empirical law, its underlying physics is well known for many relaxation phenomena such as luminescence decay in condensed phases [16]. Non-exponential relaxation indicates that the barriers between the manifold states in a free energy surface are relatively low or inexistent (compared with k B T).

Given the intrinsic heterogeneous nature of ILs and IL-like systems such as solvate-ILs [17], it is not surprising that dynamical aspects of the solvation process or the solvent dynamics exhibit multi- or non-exponential behaviour on the order of 100 ns [18]. For example, solvation and rotation dynamics of coumarin 153 in a series of ILs based on trihexyl(tetradecyl)phosphonium cation [P66614]+ revealed a highly non-exponential behaviour, characterized by stretched exponential functions with exponents ranging between 0.4 and 0.6 [19]. Also, the rotation dynamics of solutes in ILs is influenced by its shape, size and electrostatic interactions [20]. In highly concentrated aqueous ionic solutions and supercooled liquids approaching to the glass transition, the time correlation function transients are usually described by a KWW function rather than the exponential decays in diluted solutions [21]. As these highly concentrated electrolytes containing Li salts can perform as ILs and supercooled liquids at room temperature, it is of great interest investigate the dynamical behaviour of such systems in order to formulate relationships with their solution properties. Thus, in this work we employed molecular dynamics simulations to explore the solvation of sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures going to [LiBF4] up to ca. 6 mol.L−1, a well-characterized system from the experimental point of view (Chart 1 ). First, we start examining some structural aspects of the mixtures, giving an overview of how the solvation environment around Li+ ions changes as a function of the molar fraction of sulfolane. Then, we discuss some findings about the dynamical aspects of the sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures, namely the first steps of the relaxation process of the cation-anion and cation-sulfolane pairs. We observed that the solution dynamics of the sulfolane-LiBF4 system deviate significantly from the exponential law at high electrolyte concentrations, being well described by KWW functions in such conditions. In addition, the extension of the departure from the exponential behaviour seems to reflect the aggregation of the ion pair in solution and the Li+-ion transport properties.

Sulfolane and LiBF4 compounds were modelled using the non-polarizable OPLS-AA force field [22], which predicts the correct structure and a qualitatively correct dynamics with low computational cost in comparison with other polarizable force fields [23]. The scaling factor of α = 0.8 was applied to the partial charges of Li+ and BF4 − to improve the ion mobility, since the initial MD test runs indicated possible formation of glassy-state systems due to the overestimation of the Coulomb interactions with the fixed point-charge model. The rationale behind the scaling charges is to account for partial charge transfer and electronic polarization effects that effectively shield the electrostatic interactions in condensed phase [24,25].

The procedure for the MD simulations at 303 K was as follows. First, low density initial boxes were generated using Packmol and fftool software [26,27]. Then, to bring the simulation boxes to a liquid-like density, a short MD stage consisting of 105 steps of 2 fs duration in Nosé-Hoover NpT ensemble were carried out by the DL_POLY 2.20 package [28]. After, the systems were transferred to the GROMACS package [29–33] for further annealing and equilibration runs. The simulations were performed in cubic simulation boxes with periodic boundary conditions in all directions. The MD simulations at the production stage employed the Nosé-Hoover NpT ensemble at 303 K and p = 1 atm (relaxation times of 0.5 ps and 4.0 ps for thermostat and barostat, respectively), velocity Verlet integration with timesteps of 1 fs, distance cutoff of 1.60 nm, particle mesh Ewald method with B-spline interpolation of order five, grid spacing for fast Fourier transforms of 0.10 (equilibration and production), and accuracy of Ewald sum kept at 5 × 10−6 at the cutoff (equilibration and production). Also, the production stages in NpT at 303 K were 50 ns long, with trajectories stored each 2 fs in the last 10 ns.

The diffusion coefficients of the species were also evaluated at 355 K through the mean square displacements. This temperature was chosen because the calculated transport properties fall within the same timescales of the experimental results at 303 K. First, the NpT simulation boxes were re-equilibrated by 40 ns at 355 K. Then, an NVT production stage was carried out over 150 ns, with trajectories stored every 2 fs. The number sulfolane molecules, LiBF4 pairs, final box length at 303 K, experimental and calculated densities for sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures are presented in Table 1 .

Pair correlation functions g(r) and ion aggregation analysis were performed with in-house developed software [34]. The total X-ray structure factors, S(q), were calculated according to the methodology formerly described [35]. Pair autocorrelation functions were evaluated with Travis package [36–38]. The ionic volumes of Li+ and BF4 − species were calculated with the Gaussian software package [39] using the mPW1PW91 functional with the 6-31+G(d) basis set. These ab initio volumes computed inside an isosurface with a contour of 0.001 electrons/bohr3 density are evaluated with a numerical Monte Carlo integration algorithm, which introduces some degree of uncertainty. Thus, for a more quantitative outcome the computations were repeated over 30 times for each species, with very tight convergence in the self-consistent field procedure, and tight convergence in the volume integration.

We start introducing the structural aspects of the mixtures such as pair distribution functions for key centres, coordination number analysis, neighbour matrix analysis, structure factors, and aggregation analysis. The goal is to give an overview of how the environment around Li+ cations is changing as a function of the molar fraction of sulfolane.

Fig. 1 depicts the pair distribution functions for some selected atoms representing the polar regions in sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures. The g(r)s were calculated from MD simulation trajectories stored over 10 ns at 303 K. The peak maxima in Li-O and Li-F pair distribution functions are placed respectively at 0.20 and 0.21 nm. The Li-O profiles do not significantly change with the sulfolane mole fraction (χ SL). With respect to the Li-F profiles, the intensities of the first and second g(r) peaks follow the increase in LiBF4 content, while the broad band at intermediate distance of 0.75 nm decreases. Therefore, there is a reorganization of the solution structure around the Li+ ions as the molar ratio of LiBF4 increases. Thus, one can expect two scenarios: the formation of two distinct environments for Li+ (one BF4 −-rich and other sulfolane-rich), or the existence of just one solvation environment for Li+ cations shared by sulfolane molecules and BF4 −, thus forming contact ion pairs (CIPs). As seen in the g(r) F-O, the decrease of the distance between F atoms of BF4 − and O atoms of sulfolane SO2 groups upon addition of electrolyte supports the second hypothesis. Raman spectroscopy studies of LiBF4/sulfone-based mixtures (dimethyl sulfone, ethyl methyl sulfone, ethyl isopropyl sulfone, sulfolane, and 3-methylsulfolane) also concluded that BF4 − should be involved in the coordination of multiple Li+ ions in solutions with molar ratio [sulfone]/[LiBF4] ≤ 2, once the coordination number of Li+ cations cannot be satisfied solely by the sulfone molecules present in solution [6,7].

The corresponding integrated g(r)s of Li-O and Li-F upon to r = 0.8 nm are displayed in Fig. 2 . From these curves one can estimate the coordination numbers (CN) of sulfolane molecules and BF4 − anions around Li+ ions at the end of the first solvation shell (ca. 0.29 nm, which represents the first minima in the respective pair distribution functions). At diluted solutions of LiBF4, the ionic pair is fully dissociated with virtually no BF4 − anions present in the vicinity of the Li+. The CN numbers remain almost constant in the χ SL range 0.98–0.89. The evolution in Li-F coordination numbers as the concentration of BF4 − is increased indicates a gradual displacement of the sulfolane molecules from the first solvation shell around the Li+ to accommodate the incoming anions. At χ SL = 0.57 (corresponding to a molar ratio sulfolane/LiBF4 = 1.33), the CN is ca. 2.6 and 2.4 for Li-sulfolane and Li-BF4 − pairs, respectively.

One can picture the Li+ ions acting as nodes of a network connecting sulfolane molecules and BF4 − anions. The connectivity matrices shown in Fig. 3 help to better understand the organization of the first solvation shell around Li+ cations (the connectivity matrices for all χ SL are shown in Fig. S1 in Supporting Information). The criteria used for accounting the SL—Li+ and BF4 −—Li+ bonding events were again the first minimum in Li-O and Li-F pair distribution functions, ca. 0.29 nm in both cases. At χ SL = 0.95, ca. 7% of the Li+ cations have a BF4 − anion present in its first solvation shell. As the electrolyte concentration increases, the local environment around the Li+ cations becomes more heterogeneous, with formation of contact ion pairs. Despite the complexity pointed out by the local distributions of sulfolane and BF4 −, the results do not indicate, for instance, that the Li+ population is segregated in distinct solvation environments such as one sulfolane-rich and another BF4 −-rich. Instead, there is a continuum of possible combinations for solvation shells around Li+ ions in sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures, with an average CN between 4 and 5, and with the majority of Li+ cations placed in the middle of these distributions.

In equimolar mixtures of small oligoethers (triglyme G3 or tetraglyme G4) with lithium salts such as Li[NTf2], the glyme molecules are able to form stable, long-lived complexes of the type [Li(glyme)1]+ [40]. Venn diagrams from MD results indicated that more than 90% of all glyme molecules in solution are coordinated with Li+ ions using all O atoms, i.e., glymes are able to dissociate the Li+-NTf2 − contact ion pair. Also, the average O ligands per Li+ ion found at 298 K was 5.6, against 1.8 for the Li-Oanion [40].

Interestingly, a negligible fraction of sulfolane molecules is bound bidentately to a single Li+ cation (ca. 0.1%, as indicated in Table S1 in Supporting Information), while ca. 12% of bound BF4 − are in bidentate mode with Li+ at χ SL = 0.98, and increases to about 23% in the other end of the concentration range studied, at χ SL = 0.57. Also, because of the strong electrostatic interactions between Li+ and anions [1], there is ca. 1% of tridentate biding of BF4 − to the Li+ cations at χSL = 0.57. Table S2 in Supporting Information shows the percentage of SL molecules and BF4 − ions coordinated to Li+ as a function of the χ SL. In the highly concentrated electrolyte regimes, virtually all sulfolane molecules and BF4 − ions present in solution are participating in the solvation process of the lithium cation, with a negligible proportion of free, uncoordinated solvent molecules [1].

As stated before, the coordination analysis pointed out a residual amount of Li+ ions coordinated with two O atoms of the same sulfolane SO2 group. On the other hand, the approximation of a second Li+ to an O atom already coordinated with a first Li+ ion is not energetically favourable because of the coulombic repulsion between the cations. Thus, the fraction of O atoms bounded simultaneously to more than one Li+ ion remains low in the entire concentration range studied (Table S3 in Supporting Information). Since at low χ SL all sulfolane molecules are contributing to the solvation of the Li+ and there is no O atoms coordinating with two Li+ ions, the results suggest that the two oxygen atoms of the SO2 group are involved in the bridging coordination of two different Li+ cations [1]. In the case of BF4 −, the increase in electrolyte concentration leads to a gradual growth of the cation-anion polar network (discussed below), since BF4 − ions start replacing SL molecules in the Li+ solvation shell and forming contact ion pairs. Trace amounts of single F atoms coordinated simultaneously with two different cations are present in diluted LiBF4 solutions but represent ca. 6% of the total in χ SL = 0.57.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total and partial X-ray structure factors
ce:section.ce:para: The total X-ray structure factors S(q) up to q = 20 nm−1 for the studied LiBF4—sulfolane solutions are presented in Fig. 4 a. Overall, there are three distinct peaks present that will be discussed below in detail. The whole S(q) profile of these highly concentrated electrolytes resembles that seen by X-ray scattering (combined WAXS/SAXS), high energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) and neutron diffraction experiments in room-temperature ionic liquids with large nonpolar groups [41–50]. The S(q) at χ SL = 0.98 in Fig. 4a exhibits a single peak at the region associated with adjacency correlations between neighbouring atoms (12 ≤ q ≤ 18 nm−1), with the characteristic profile of pure sulfolane, and which is very similar to other aprotic solvents [41,42]. As the concentration of LiBF4 increases, there is a rise of the S(q) region at ca. 6 nm−1 (from Bragg relationship, 2*π/6 = 1.05 nm in direct space) that reaches a maximum about χ SL = 0.75 and then decreases again. This small prepeak indicates polar–nonpolar (p/np) alternation regime and is linked to structural nanoscale heterogeneity of the solution. The polar domain in the mixtures consists of Li+ and BF4 − ions and the SO2 group of sulfolane, while nonpolar domain contains CH2 groups of sulfolane. The inspection of the atomic pair subcomponents revealed that the prepeak contributions comes mainly from positive SO2—SO2 and negative SO2—CH2 cross correlations (Fig. 4b). The SO2—CH2 anti-peak reaches its minimum at χ SL = 0.67.

As the molar fraction of sulfolane decreases from χ SL 0.75 to 0.57, an intermediate band arises at ca. 9.8 nm−1 (0.65 nm in direct space), higher than the prepeak but lower than the adjacency peak. A peak in this region between adjacency and polarity prepeak is a hallmark of charge alternation in molten salts and ionic liquids. The total S(q) can be represented as weighted contributions of partial structure factors. In ionic liquids, for example, careful partitioning of S(q) into its subcomponents can provide a wealth of information about the structural features at different length scales [9,41,42,49,50].

To investigate the origins of the intermediate peak in these highly concentrated liquid electrolytes, the total X-ray S(q) function was partitioned into the contributions of Li+, BF4 − and sulfolane:

and the partial S(q) functions for some representative sulfolane molar fractions are shown in Fig. 4c. The reduced scattering X-ray form factor of Li+ cation has little effect on the total S(q) and thus its contribution to partial S(q) are very small, making it difficult to infer any structural features directly from its cross correlations. As stated before, the positive peaks represent homogeneous correlations (+/+, -/-, p/p, np/np) and negative peaks, or antipeaks, denote heterogeneous correlations (+/-, p/np). Overall, the partial S(q) showed that the profile of the total S(q) is mainly dictated by the sulfolane solvent organization up to χ SL = 0.67. The low intensity of the BF4 −-based subcomponents at χ SL = 0.95 indicates that the anions (and therefore the cations) are randomly distributed in solution. As the concentration of LiBF4 increases, the changes in the low-q region points to a rearrangement of the polar domains of the sulfolane to accommodate the incoming ions, therefore forming the polar-nonpolar network. At intermediate q range of 10 nm−1 corresponding to the charge alternation, BF4 −—BF4 − and sulfolane—sulfolane correlations contribute to a peak, while the antipeak contribution of the BF4 −—sulfolane cross correlation is an indirect indication of the intermediacy of Li+ cations.

In other concentrated organic lithium electrolytes such as equimolar mixtures of glymes G3 or G4 with salts Li[NTf2], Li[CF3CO2] and Li[NO3], the WAXS and HEXRD revealed the presence of the three bands, but the intermediate peak is incipient [51]. The neutron diffraction data of concentrated LiPF6 and LiClO4 in propylene carbonate showed only the intermediate and adjacency peaks [52,53], since the spatial dimension of the nonpolar part is not large enough to disrupt the polar network. Also, experimental WAXS of concentrated Li[NTf2]/acetonitrile solutions exhibit only the two peaks with larger q values [54]. The three peaks can also be seen in scattering profiles of super-high-concentrated water-in-salt electrolytes. In mixtures such as 21 mol/kg of Li[NTf2] in H2O, with strong cation–anion interactions, the combined SAXS/WAXS and neutron scattering experiments revealed that the origin of the first sharp diffraction peak placed at 1.3 nm is the phase separation of the water molecules from the ions [4,55]. Thus, the manifestation of the prepeak will depend on a global symmetry breaking of the charge ordering in solution [56], induced by strong ion-ion or solvent-solvent associations, or by the presence of neutral groups bonded to the ions large enough to upset the global charge order.

The connectivity analysis of the solution network have shown that effectively all sulfolane molecules and BF4 − ions present are involved in the solvation process of the Li+ cations in the highly concentrated electrolyte regimes.1 Also, the connectivity matrix distributions indicate that aggregates such as [Li x (SL) y (BF4) z ] x
−
z (where x = 1 or more) might be formed in solution [1]. Fig. 5 portrays the aggregation analysis between Li+ ions and sulfolane molecules, as well as between Li+ cations and BF4 − anions, for some selected sulfolane-LiBF4 compositions. Here, the distance threshold used for accounting the aggregation was the first minima in g(r)s of Li-S and Li-B (0.415 and 0.455 nm, respectively). The central S and B atoms were chosen because they are proper representations of averaged positions for O and F atoms. The neighbour search method employed was network structures of the type A-B-A-B, where a solvent molecule (or BF4 − ion) is shared between two Li+ cations. In sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures up to χ SL = 0.80, the Li+ cations are completely solvated by the solvent and are mainly isolated or forming variously sized clusters with few Li+ ions. The Fig. S2a and S2b in Supporting Information show the cluster size distributions for cation-sulfolane and cation-anion aggregates, respectively. The evolution of the aggregate sizes must follow a power law of the form:
(2)
P
n
=
cn
- τ
where c is a constant, n is the aggregate size, and τ is the percolation exponent. For large values of n at the percolation threshold, the asymptotic values of τ are respectively 2.05 and 2.19 for two- and three-dimensional percolation [57]. The power law above is not valid for small n. Also, because of the finite-size effects, clusters approaching to the system size tend to be overrepresented [58] exhibiting a hump in P(n) at large n values. Thus, only a range of the values can be fitted by Eq. (2), and the percolation threshold will be situated near to the χ SL fitted with the widest data array. For cation-sulfolane aggregates, the critical percolation concentration is reached around χ SL = 0.75 (fit from n = 8 to n = 121, τ = 2.00). The experimental evidence along with the MD results discussed so far show that the two O atoms of the sulfone group are coordinated with two different Li ions in the mixtures at high electrolyte concentrations. With respect to the Li+-BF4 − network, aggregation analysis exhibits a gradual increase in cation-anion aggregate size with the electrolyte content. The critical percolation concentration in this case is reached between χ SL 0.57–0.67 (τ = 1.75 for the fit from n = 6 to n = 369 at χ SL = 0.57). This finding corroborates the experimental observations of Raman B-F symmetric stretching vibration mode of the BF4 − anion shifting to higher wavenumbers, which suggests that the interaction between Li+ and BF4 − is becoming stronger with the increase in LiBF4 concentration [6]. Furthermore, despite some finite-size effects influencing the percolation analysis as described above (with an overrepresentation of P(n) for medium-sized clusters as well), the overall results indicate essentially a two-dimensional percolation rather than a 3-dimensional one in both cases. Also, in the case of the cation-anion aggregation probability functions, the results at χ SL = 0.57 reveal that the clusters span over a wide range of aggregate sizes, suggesting an inhomogeneous distribution of the ion clusters in solution. Summarizing the aggregation results, above the critical percolation concentration reached between χ SL 0.57 and 0.67, network structures like Li+—SL—Li+—SL and Li+—BF4 −—Li+—BF4 − are formed. The cation-cation aggerate distribution (Fig. S3 in Supporting Information) follows closely the pattern seen for the cation-solvent and will not be further discussed.

Fig. 6 depicts snapshots of the MD simulation boxes at T = 303 K for sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures near the critical percolation concentrations, i.e., in χ SL 0.57–0.80 M fraction range. In these sampling snapshots, only events where a Li+ cation is shared simultaneously by O and F atoms are highlighted. The distance criteria here were the same used previously in evaluation of the connectivity matrices. The overall picture is that at high molar ratio of electrolyte content the Li+ ions are interlinked as nodes of a complex and dynamic network formed by sulfolane and BF4 − anions.

In this second part we discuss some findings about the dynamical aspects of the sulfolane-LiBF4 mixtures. We presented ab initio calculations showing the differences between the radii of solvated and unsolvated species and associated these differences with a change in the diffusion mechanism of the Li+. Then, we calculate the Li+–BF4 − and Li+–sulfolane dimer autocorrelation function and demonstrate that the transients follow a non-exponential behaviour. The decay times are related to the reciprocal of diffusion, as expected, and the stretching exponent seems to correlate with the cation-anion aggregation analysis. The stretching exponent is working as an indicator of the heterogeneity of the mixture.

Pulse-field gradient NMR measurements indicated faster Li-ion diffusion than sulfolane and BF4 − in concentrated electrolytes [1] . Fig. 7 presents the experimental data [1] for ratios of self-diffusion coefficients D SL/D Li and D BF4/D Li together with the values obtained by MD simulations at T = 355 K in this work. The self-diffusion coefficients calculated through mean square displacements at T = 303 and 355 K are shown in Table S4 in SI, together with experimental values [1] determined at 303 K. The ratios calculated at 355 K are in line with the results of MD simulations carried out at T = 303 K (Fig. S4 in Supporting Information), despite of the much slower diffusion coefficients obtained at this later temperature, as anticipated for classical non-polarizable force fields. Overall, there is a good agreement between the experimental and calculated trends, indicating that the ratios D SL/D Li and D BF4/D Li become lower than 1 with χ SL ≤ 0.75. The Stokes-Einstein (S-E) equation is represented by:
(3)
D =
k B
T
C
π η R
h
where k B T is the Boltzmann constant multiplied by the temperature, η is the solution viscosity, R h is the Stokes radius (hydrodynamic radius) and C is a constant between 4 and 6 determined by slip and stick boundary conditions, respectively [59]. Based on the experimental D Li at χ SL = 0.91 [1], the Stokes radius of Li+ (0.28 nm) is in line with the anion (0.25 nm) but definitely larger than the solvent (0.21 nm). As a comparison, the ionic radii of Li+ and BF4 − calculated by DFT (0.098 and 0.26 nm, respectively) indicates that the cation travels as a solvated complex [Li(SL) x ]+ in diluted electrolyte solutions, while the anion species drags much less solvent in such conditions. At the other end of the concentration range, DLi and DBF4 face a 30- and 50-fold reduction, respectively, suggesting that the anion is being subjected to a caging dynamics in highly structured solvent realms (as pointed out by the aggregation analysis), while Li+ undergoes less local friction, despite of both ions are sharing the same polar regions of the solution. Also, the Stokes radius of Li+ extrapolated to χ SL = 0.57 with the S-E equation would be 0.073 nm (using the experimental viscosities and self-diffusion coefficients at 303 K [1]), pointing to a diffusion of the Li+ striped off its solvation shell. Clearly, the Li+ transport mechanism experiences a change from caging to hopping with the increase in electrolyte concentration.

The solution structure pictured by the aggregation analysis indicated an inhomogeneous distribution of the ions, with the behaviour of the system dictated by a combination of free ions (surrounded by solvent molecules only), solvent-separated ion pairs (SSIPs), contact ion pairs (CIPs) and aggregates. Thus, it is of particular interest to examine the effect of the local heterogeneities on the solvation dynamics in highly concentrated electrolyte regimes. The relaxation lifetimes of the pairs Li+–BF4 − and Li–sulfolane were evaluated at 355 K over trajectories of 150 ns employing the following dimer autocorrelation function (DACF) [36,37]:

where M and N depicts respectively the number of centres i and j analysed, and Sij (t) = 1 if a pair ij is within the distance threshold in step t and zero otherwise. The distance criterium for cation-anion and cation-sulfolane was defined from the first minima (0.29 nm) in the corresponding g(r)s presented in Fig. 1. It was adopted the continuous formalism for accounting the DACFs, where the contact pair ij is considered definitely separated when one of the species reaches the border of the hydration shell (the distance threshold). If the pair reforms, even being formed from the same two centres it will be counted as a new pair [36,37].

The semi-log curves of the continuous DACFs evaluated at T = 355 K and shown in Fig. 8 follow a non-exponential behaviour. The upward curvature is more evident for high electrolyte concentrations, with the residence time of the anion near the Li+ increasing with the reduction in χ SL. The transients were fitted with the Kohlrausch–Williams–Watts function
C
t
= exp
-
t
τ 0
β
KWW
, where the stretching exponent β KWW is in the range 0 < β KWW ≤ 1 and τ 0 has dimensions of time. The best fitting parameters for the relaxation kinetics of the dimers are listed in Table 2 . The parameter τ 0 can be interpreted as an estimate time of BF4 − ions or sulfolane molecules in the solvation shell of Li+ Also, τ 0 provides a timescale by which the relaxation process evolves. However, it is important to stress that τ 0 does not represent an average of τ values nor the inverse of the average time [60]. In these mixtures with high electrolyte concentrations where interionic forces play a major role, charge and density fluctuation frequencies should be quite similar, and the diffusive pathways are not expected to be distinct. In both cases τ 0 increases as the χ SL decreases since the solvated Li+ ions become more encircled by BF4 − anions, and the viscosity of the mixtures also increases. The dynamics were calculated at 355 K because at this temperature the results fall in the same timescale of the experimental data at 303 K, facilitating the comparisons. The survival times τ 0 calculated for the LI+–BF4 − CIP and the cation-sulfolane pairs are placed in sub-ns and ns range, respectively, indicating that the Li+ ion quickly exchanges SL and BF4 − ligands even in highly concentrated liquid electrolytes with increased viscosity. As a comparison, the water exchange rate on the first coordination shell of Li+ and the lifetime of monodentate-type ligand exchange on Li+ are within the nanosecond time scale [61].

The Travis software package has two options to calculate Dimer ACFs: “continuous” and “intermittent”. The lifetimes seem in Table 2 result from the “continuous” mode in Travis as described above. These lifetimes seem to indicate the residence time of Li+ in the solvation shell. The mode “intermittent” – where a reformed pair is always accounted as the same pair (see Figure S5 in SI) – gives much longer lifetimes highly correlated with viscosity. Thus, combining the two pieces of information, Li+ ions can quickly switch ligands, but stay long periods in the vicinity of the same ligands.

The β KWW coefficients of Li+-BF4 − and Li-sulfolane relaxations are highly correlated (R 2 = 0.998). The parameter β KWW for sulfolane-Li+ pair shifts from 0.94 in diluted solutions to 0.58 in χ SL = 0.57, while β KWW for the Li+–BF4 − ion pair reaches 0.45 at the same conditions. In addition, β KWW coefficients assuming values significantly below unit represent an expression of the glassy character of the systems, with the relaxation phenomena taking place in heterogeneous, amorphous medium. Thus, as the molar ratio sulfolane:LiBF4 decreases to ca. 2, the solvation environment around Li+ cations become more heterogeneous, inducing some local charge imbalance, and consequently expanding the configuration space by which Li+ can jump to the next available solvation cage. Consequently, the relaxation of the Li+ cation alters its Coulomb interactions with the surrounding ions [14]. At the high end of the [LiBF4] (χ SL 0.57–0.67), where both aggregate networks sharing the Li+ ions are percolated and the solution viscosity is high, the relaxation dynamics of the mixtures can be compared with that seen on glasses. The Scher-Lax-Phillips (SLP) model for glassy dynamics [14,62] states that β KWW(T) = β KWW(T g) at the glass transition temperature, T g, and that the relaxation process is given by:
(5)
β
KWW
(
T g
)
= d /
( d + 2 / f )
where d is the dimensionality of the configuration space, and f is the number of short-range forces divided by the total number of forces. The model predicts for a relaxation process dominated by short-range Brownian forces (f = 1) β SR = 3/5 = 0.60 at T g, which has correspondence with the β values of the Li-sulfolane pair, indicating that density fluctuations play a central role in the diffusive relaxation mechanism of the pair. Additionally, the SLP model anticipates β LR = 3/7 = 0.43 for a process dominated by long-range Coulomb forces (f = 0.5), in line with the β KWW values found for the cation-anion dimer, pointing out that the relaxation dynamics of the Li+–BF4 − CIP is regulated by charge fluctuations. Thus, the results provided by the MD simulations suggest that the relaxation behaviour of LiBF4-sulfolane mixtures at room or even higher temperatures is somewhat analogous to that seen in glass-forming materials at much lower temperatures, and that the local heterogeneities around the Li+ ion triggered by BF4 − and sulfolane competition result in the dynamical response of the mixtures.

Here we explore the relationships with the Stokes-Einstein equation. The idea is to identify what relaxation process is the largest contributor to the S-E deviations. Also, going beyond the S-E relationships, we extend the correlations to the lithium transference number. The Li+ ion dynamics in salt-sulfolane mixtures is influenced to such an extent that the hopping diffusion of Li+ ions starts to take place in highly concentrated electrolyte solutions [10]. Also, the escape of the Li+ from the solvation cage in these mixtures is controlled by density and charge fluctuations, involving the concerted rearrangement of a number of particles in the vicinity of the cation. A convenient framework to interpret these results is in terms of fast, intramolecular or single particle dynamics, known as β relaxations, leading the slow, collective diffusive motions called α relaxations [63,64]. Hence, within this framework one can establish associations between the relaxation dynamics of the mixtures and its macroscopic properties. We first looked at the relationships of Li+—sulfolane and Li+—BF4 − relaxation times with the S-E equation. For KWW functions, the average relaxation time <τ> is defined as [65]:

where Γ is the Euler’s gamma function. The deviations of D ∼ <τ>−
ξ from ξ = 1 are smaller for the Li+–sulfolane pair, as well as for η ∼ <τ> ξ (Figs. S6, S7 and S8 in Supporting information). In general, it was found fairly good correlations for both relaxation processes despite of the departure of ξ = 1. The corresponding relationships D ∼ <τ>−
ξ for the self-diffusion coefficients evaluated by MD at 355 K were presented in Figs. S9 and S10 in Supporting Information, for comparison. The same general observations can be extended to the calculated diffusion coefficients, with the Li+—BF4 − relaxation dynamics displaying the highest deviations from ξ = 1.

The combined results suggest that the largest contributor to the deviations from ξ = 1 in LiBF4–sulfolane mixtures is the cation-anion pair dynamics, which is a direct consequence of the electrostatic interactions between the ions. In equimolar mixtures of triglyme G3 with LiCF3CO2, LiNO3 and Li[NTf2], for example [51], the strong electrostatic interaction between Li+ and NO3 – (respectively, a hard Lewis acid and a hard Lewis base) prevents the effective separation of the ion pair by the solvent. In LiBF4–sulfolane mixtures, the low nucleophilicity of the tetrafluoroborate anion allows Li+ ions to quickly exchange ligands even at high electrolyte concentrations, with eventual Li+ hopping conduction and consequent decoupling between diffusion and viscosity.

The net mobility of the Li+ ion is an important parameter for its use in lithium-based battery cells, since only the cation undergoes redox reactions on electrodes. The transference number t i is used to evaluate the conducting performance of the cation. Electrolyte systems with transference numbers approaching to 1 produce high power density. To reduce eventual sources of error associated with the diffusion coefficients calculated from MD trajectories, the transference number of Li+, t Li, (Table S5 in SI) was estimated from experimental [1] diffusion coefficients of Li+ and BF4 −:

Thus, it should be interesting to evaluate the existence of correlations between the ionic conductivity and these primary relaxation steps. Fig. 9 shows the plot of the experimental values [1] of t Li as a function of the average rate constant <k>, calculated as 1/<τ> from eq. (6). There is a good linear correlation of t Li at different χ SL with <k> on both relaxation processes, indicating that t Li seems to reach a limiting value of ca. 0.62 as the average rate constants of Li+–sulfolane and Li+–BF4 − relaxations approach to 0. The experimental data [1] of the LiBF4–sulfolane mixtures shown in Fig. 9b indicates the t Li reaches its limiting value at χ SL between 0.57 and 0.67. The same correlations with t Li values from the self-diffusion coefficients calculated by MD at T = 355 K are presented in Fig. S11 in SI for comparison (in this case we are unable to clearly point out the χ SL range for a maximum t Li value because of some sensitivity loss at this temperature). The rate constant <k> is intrinsically correlated with β KWW. Thus, in terms of β KWW parameters, at the diluted regime (i.e., highest <k> values, β KWW → 1) the predicted t Li value is in line with experimental data for lithium electrolytes in nonaqueous solvents (0.38–0.48 range) [6,66]. At the other end, t Li seems to reach a maximum where charge fluctuations (β KWW → 0.43 in the case of Li+–BF4 − CIP) and density fluctuations (β KWW → 0.60 in Li+–sulfolane pair) dominates the respective relaxation process.

The results provided an intricate picture of the structure and dynamics of LiBF4–sulfolane mixtures. As the electrolyte concentration increases, the solvent content is not enough to keep the ions separated in their respective solvation shells, giving rise to the formation of solvent-separated ion pairs (SSIPs), contact ion pairs (CIPs) and large aggregates. Thus, the physical and chemical response of the system will be regulated by this inhomogeneous distribution of the ions in solution. As the concentration of the salt increases and reaches the critical percolation thresholds (χ SL between 0.80 and 0.75 for cation-sulfolane aggregates, and χ SL between 0.67 and 0.57 for cation-anion aggregates), the fast processes involving single-particle dynamics of Li+ ions and solvent reorganization resembles that of glass-forming materials at much lower temperatures where T is near T g. The ion transport in these highly concentrated electrolyte environments reflects the dynamics of the primary relaxation steps between cation-anion and cation-solvent, changing from vehicular-type mechanism to jump diffusion. At fully percolated conditions, i.e., in the presence of a continuous network between cations and anions, as well as between cations and the solvent, the relaxation of the contact ion pair is dictated by long-range Coulomb interactions, while density fluctuations seem to play an important role in Li+–sulfolane pair relaxation. The Coulomb component is the major contributor to the departure of the solution properties from the Stokes-Einstein relationship, since its displacement within the local cluster or jump to an available solvation cage involves a cooperative interaction with other ions in the vicinity. The formation of these two different continuous networks sharing the Li+-ion in LiBF4–sulfolane mixtures seems to reach a sweet spot at ca. χ SL = 0.67 where the transference number of the lithium takes its maximum value. We hope that our results revealing the role played by the primary relaxation steps of Li ions in HCE solutions can lead to innovational batteries that can contribute to construct a sustainable society.
